place: Castorland village, New York, New York

facts.text: Castorland’s name comes from the French word 'castor' for beaver, chosen because early settlers prized the region’s abundant beaver pelts.
In the late 1700s, French émigrés fleeing the Revolution founded Castorland, but a 1795 drowning and a 1797 law barred them from land ownership.
By 1894, Castorland’s Shantz mill and Timmerman Brothers factory shipped 137 carloads of lumber, employing over 400 workers.
Today, the Castorland Hotel stands as a preserved reminder of the village’s lumber‑era past.
The area that is now Castorland was once part of the Oneida and Mohawk nations’ stewardship along the Black River valley.
Castorland belongs to the Watertown‑Fort Drum Metropolitan Statistical Area, linking it to Lewis and Jefferson counties.
